mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Romania on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe — south-eastern Europe, on the Black Sea. 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Romania?
mapQuestions.answer: Bucharest — sometimes called "Little Paris" for its 1900s architecture.

mapQuestions.question: What sea touches Romania?
mapQuestions.answer: The Black Sea, on the east. 🌊

mapQuestions.question: What mountain range runs across Romania?
mapQuestions.answer: The Carpathian Mountains — they curve through the country like a giant horseshoe.

mapQuestions.question: What is the historic region of Transylvania famous for?
mapQuestions.answer: Medieval fortified Saxon villages, the Carpathian Mountains, and being the setting of the Dracula legend.

foods.name: Mămăligă
foods.description: Yellow cornmeal porridge — Romania's answer to mashed potato. Served with sour cream, melted cheese, or alongside stew.

foods.name: Sarmale
foods.description: Cabbage rolls stuffed with pork mince and rice, slow-cooked with bacon and tomato. The classic Romanian dinner.

foods.name: Ciorbă
foods.description: A sour soup — many varieties, but the most common is ciorbă de burtă (tripe soup) or ciorbă de perișoare (meatball soup with vegetables).

foods.name: Plăcintă
foods.description: A flat baked pie filled with cheese, apples, pumpkin, or cabbage. Found in every Romanian bakery.

foods.name: Mititei
foods.description: Small grilled minced-meat rolls, eaten with mustard and bread. Popular at markets and street food stalls.

foods.name: Cozonac
foods.description: A sweet bread filled with walnuts, raisins, cocoa, or Turkish delight. Made for Christmas, Easter, and any big occasion.

scavengerItems.label: A castle or fortified church on a hill
A wolf or bear road-warning sign
An Orthodox church with onion domes
A primeval forest with very old trees
A horse-drawn cart on a country road
A shepherd with a flock of sheep
A brightly painted traditional wooden Romanian house
A wooden Maramureș church with a tall spire
A souvenir featuring Vlad the Impaler or Dracula
A photo of Nadia Comăneci or a gymnastics statue

geoMatches.description: The capital. Grand boulevards, the world's heaviest building (the Palace of the Parliament), and a wild nightlife scene.
A medieval Transylvanian town under the Carpathian mountains, with a 14th-century black-walled church and skiing on its doorstep.
A medieval cliff-top castle marketed as "Dracula's Castle". Pretty, atmospheric, and full of bats.
A perfectly preserved Saxon town in Transylvania. Quirk: many roof windows look like eyes peering out at the streets.
Where the Danube River meets the Black Sea — a vast wetland with hundreds of bird species, pelicans, and remote fishing villages.

senses.smell: Woodsmoke from village chimneys, sheep cheese, wild garlic, mountain pine, fresh bread…
senses.hear: Orthodox church bells, sheep bells in the mountains, "Bună ziua!" greetings, traditional music with the pan-flute (nai)…
senses.taste: Tangy sour soup, salty sheep cheese, dark plum brandy (țuică), creamy mămăligă, sweet poppy-seed cake…
senses.feel: Cobblestones underfoot, cold mountain rivers, rough wool blankets, smooth black smoked-clay pots…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Was Dracula real?
stories.body: The legend of Dracula was invented in 1897 by Irish author Bram Stoker, but he based his villain partly on a real Romanian prince called Vlad III, known as "Vlad the Impaler". Vlad ruled the region of Wallachia (just south of Transylvania) in the 1400s and was famous for impaling his enemies on long spikes — possibly thousands of them. He was a brutal but effective ruler who held off the Ottoman Empire when no-one else could. He never lived in Bran Castle (the one sold to tourists as "Dracula's Castle"), but he probably visited once. Romanians have complicated feelings about Vlad — to some he's a national hero, to others a monster.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Vlad killed thousands of people but protected his country. Is it possible to be a hero AND a monster at the same time?

stories.question: Why is one of the world's biggest buildings in Bucharest?
stories.body: The Palace of the Parliament in Bucharest is the world's heaviest building (4 million tonnes), the largest civilian building, and the most expensive administrative building ever built. It was constructed in the 1980s on the orders of communist dictator Nicolae Ceaușescu, who bulldozed an entire historic neighbourhood (40,000 people displaced, 23 churches destroyed) to build it. The building has 1,100 rooms, 480 chandeliers, and uses so much electricity it has its own power plant. Ceaușescu never actually got to use it — he was overthrown and executed in 1989, when the building was still unfinished.

stories.question: Where are there still villages from the Middle Ages?
stories.body: Maramureș is a remote region in northern Romania, bordering Ukraine. Time slows down here. Horse-drawn carts still outnumber cars in many villages. Hay is still made by hand. People still wear hand-stitched traditional clothes for church on Sundays. The wooden churches of Maramureș, some 700 years old, have tall spires (one is 80 m tall) and are UNESCO-listed. The "Merry Cemetery" in Săpânța is famous worldwide — gravestones are painted in bright blue with cheeky poems about the deceased. It's a totally different Romania from Bucharest.

stories.question: How did Romania escape communism?
stories.body: Romania was a communist state from 1948 to 1989, ruled for the last 24 years by Nicolae Ceaușescu — one of the most paranoid dictators in Eastern Europe. He starved his people to pay off foreign debt, banned contraception to grow the population, and built a secret police force (Securitate) that informed on everyone. In December 1989, after small protests in Timișoara, the country exploded in revolution. After several days of fighting, Ceaușescu and his wife tried to flee by helicopter, were captured, given a 90-minute trial, and executed on Christmas Day 1989. Romania's revolution was the bloodiest of any Eastern European country's — and the only one to execute its dictator. About 1,100 people died.

stories.question: Who is Nadia Comăneci?
stories.body: In 1976, at the Montreal Olympics, a 14-year-old Romanian gymnast called Nadia Comăneci was the first gymnast ever to score a perfect 10 — the scoreboard wasn't designed to display "10.0", so it had to flash "1.00" instead. She went on to score six more perfect 10s at those Games and won three gold medals. Romanians treat her as a national hero. After communism fell she defected from Romania in 1989, settled in the US, and married another Olympic gymnast Bart Conner. She's still considered one of the greatest gymnasts of all time.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Nadia was 14 when she scored a perfect 10. What does it take to be great at something while still being a kid?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Romania so far?
Bran Castle — would you stay overnight in "Dracula's Castle"?
Romania has more brown bears than any country in Europe except Russia. Are you up for spotting one?
Bucharest has wild nightlife and the world's biggest building. Transylvania has horse carts and medieval villages. Which Romania do you prefer?
What's one Romanian habit you'd like to bring home?
Romania was sealed off from the West for 40 years and is now part of the EU. Can you spot signs of both Romanias here?

wordSearch.hint: Romanian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Romania has Europe's biggest brown bear population — around 8,000 in the Carpathian mountains.

animals.name: Wild boar
animals.description: Everywhere — Carpathian forests, lowland farmland, even occasionally on Bucharest outskirts.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: Carpathian forests — listen for the roaring males in autumn.

animals.name: Horse-drawn cart pony
animals.description: Working horses still pull carts in rural Maramureș and Transylvanian villages.

animals.name: Pelican
animals.description: White pelicans and Dalmatian pelicans in the Danube Delta — Europe's biggest pelican colony.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: About 2,500 wolves in the Carpathians — biggest wolf population in Europe outside Russia.

animals.name: Eurasian lynx
animals.description: Around 1,500 lynx in the Carpathians, the largest population in central/eastern Europe.
